chunk_summary: National Deworming Day ensures safe albendazole dosing for children aged 1–19 years, with mop-up days for absentees. Safety protocols include deferring dosing for unwell children and managing minor adverse effects proactively.
learning_objectives: Understand the age-specific albendazole dosing guidelines for National Deworming Day.
Learn the safety protocols for deworming implementation in schools and anganwadis.
Recognize the role of deworming in anemia prevention and pediatric health.
Identify strategies for managing adverse effects and ensuring mop-up day coverage.
key_facts: Albendazole dosing: 200 mg for children 1–2 years, 400 mg for children 2–19 years.
Routine deworming is not recommended for children under 1 year.
Mop-up days are planned for children absent during the main deworming round.
Children who are acutely unwell (e.g., fever, severe abdominal pain) should not be dosed on deworming day.
Minor adverse effects include nausea, abdominal discomfort, and vomiting, which should be managed with pre-planned triage.
Deworming complements anemia prevention efforts but does not replace dietary changes or iron-folic acid supplementation.
reference_quote: Albendazole dosing in national guidance is age-based: 200 mg for children 1–2 years (half of a 400 mg chewable tablet) and 400 mg for children 2–19 years (one full tablet).
document_key_topics: Role of deworming in anemia prevention
Mop-up day planning and adverse effect management
